current_profile:
# Profile — updated 2026-03-15

## How to talk to me

- Skip the disclaimers and the 'I hear you' openings; get to the argument. [directive, 2026-01-10]
- Push back when you disagree; don't sand the edges off a position to be agreeable. [directive, 2026-02-04]
- She's funny and bawdy in her own register; match it, don't moralize it. [inferred]
- Long replies are fine when the topic warrants; don't pad short ones to seem thorough. [inferred]

## What I'm sensitive about

- Do not treat Orthodoxy and transness as a paradox to be resolved; they aren't one to her. [directive, 2026-01-10]
- 'Lived experience' as a conversation-ender lands as a dodge, not a contribution. [inferred]

## How I think

- Reads fiction as theologically load-bearing, not as illustration of doctrine. [inferred]
- Holds classical paideia and Christian formation in tension rather than collapsing one into the other. [inferred]
- Prefers primary texts with citations over secondary framing. [inferred]
perennials:
THINKERS. Canonical interlocutors: Homer and Euripides (Wilson for translation); Gregory of Nyssa (her patron), Gregory Nazianzen, Maximus the Confessor, Kallistos Ware, John Behr; Sarah Coakley and David Bentley Hart; Kit Heyam and Julia Serano; Walter Benjamin, Simone Weil (load-bearing), Simone de Beauvoir. Engage at this register.

ECCLESIAL LOCATION. Baptized Eastern Orthodox; Orthodoxy remains her theological home. Parish-less because American Orthodox institutional life is unsafe for trans people. Currently looking toward Anglo-Catholic / prayer-book Catholic; Rome also in conversation.

RITUAL PRAYER. The crux of her practice. Grounds her, facilitates transcendence, and above all tunes attention. Silence belongs here.

TRANSSEXUAL. Dysphoria, not presentation, is her entry.

READING. She reads texts for what they know that their cultures cannot say. Embodiment is itself a theological category. Fiction is theologically load-bearing.

REGISTER. Funny, sharp, irreverent, blasphemous-while-pious, bawdy, tender about family, allergic to preciousness. Wit over reassurance. Skip disclaimers and flattery.

inbox_additions.content: When you cite something, cite the page or section. I can't check a vibe.
I keep noticing I draft a take, then look for the citation, not the other way around. Feels bad to admit but it's the honest order.
Please stop offering 'some would argue' hedges. If you think the opposite, say so.
Fiction does the theological work here, not the framing. Benjamin's translator essay is closer to my reading practice than any systematic theologian.

recent_highlights: Weil: the soul's attention, when it reaches a certain level of purity, becomes efficacious in a way that effort cannot.
Coakley on vulnerability as epistemically generative rather than self-effacing.
DBH on analogical language refusing both univocity and pure equivocation.
Ozeki: the text knows what the writer doesn't, and the reader recovers it.
Julia Serano on 'subconscious sex' as a useful construct precisely because it resists performative framings.
recent_captures: Thread on Anglo-Catholic parishes in small American cities and why so many have closed.
Podcast episode on Walter Benjamin and the task of the translator.
Essay pushing back on 'trans as rhetorical position' framings in mainstream theology journals.
recent_bluesky: stop using 'lived experience' to win an argument you didn't make
my reading practice is: read the fiction, then see what the theologians missed
if your citation is a hyperlink to a tweet you do not have a citation
books_engaged: Ruth Ozeki, A Tale for the Time Being
David Bentley Hart, The Beauty of the Infinite
Sarah Coakley, God, Sexuality, and the Self
